## Rendering: layered canvases

Three stacked `<canvas>` React components, each `React.memo` with a custom `areEqual`:
- **StaticCanvas** — committed elements (fills, strokes, text). `packages/excalidraw/components/canvases/StaticCanvas.tsx`
- **InteractiveCanvas** — selection boxes, handles, remote cursors, snap lines. Never memoized on cursor move.
- **NewElementCanvas** — the single element being actively drawn (draw-perf optimization).

Split exists so a selection change repaints only the cheap interactive layer, not every element.

## Update trigger chain

`mutateElement` (`packages/element/src/mutateElement.ts`) → `element.version++`, new `versionNonce`, **`ShapeCache.delete(element)`** (invalidates that element's cached shape) → `Scene.mutateElement` → `Scene.triggerUpdate()` sets `sceneNonce = randomInteger()` → `onUpdate` callback → App `setState` → `App.render()`.

`App.render()` → `Renderer.getRenderableElements({...})` (`scene/Renderer.ts`):
- memoized (`memoize`) on `canvasNonce = sceneNonce` — same nonce → cached result, no recompute.
- builds `elementsMap`, drops the currently-edited text element + `newElement`, then **viewport-culls** via `isElementInViewport` → `visibleElements`.

Result props flow to the canvas components. `areEqual` compares `canvasNonce / scale / elementsMap / visibleElements / appState` — unchanged → React skips, no repaint.

## Actual paint

StaticCanvas `useEffect` → `renderStaticScene` → **`renderStaticSceneThrottled` (`throttleRAF`, one paint per animation frame)** → `_renderStaticScene` (`renderer/staticScene.ts`):
1. bootstrap canvas (size, DPR scale, bg), `context.scale(zoom)`, grid,
2. per `visibleElement` → `renderElement(...)`; text-in-container and iframe-likes handled specially (embeddables drawn on top).

## Per-element cache (why it's fast) — `element/src/renderElement.ts`

Two WeakMaps keyed by element identity:
- **`ShapeCache`** (`element/src/shape.ts`) — rough.js `Drawable` geometry, checked against `theme`.
- **`elementWithCanvasCache`** — a pre-rendered **off-screen canvas per element**. `generateElementWithCanvas` regenerates only when `zoomValue`, `theme`, bound-text version, image crop, containing-frame opacity (or arrow-label angle) changed; otherwise `drawElementFromCanvas` just blits the cached bitmap.

So a scene mutation only busts the caches of the mutated elements (via `ShapeCache.delete`); everything else is copied from its cached canvas. Interactive layer redraws on its own nonces (`selectionNonce`, etc.) independent of element geometry.

Net: **identity + nonce/version invalidation → React memo gate → RAF throttle → per-element bitmap cache**, four layers that each avoid redundant work.

## Excalidraw rendering & update model

**Two-canvas split** — content vs overlay, each independently memoized.
- `StaticCanvas.tsx` → content layer: shapes/text/images/grid/frame clips. Real `<canvas>` built outside React, injected via `replaceChildren`; `useEffect` → `renderStaticScene`.
- `InteractiveCanvas.tsx` → overlay layer: selection boxes, transform/resize/rotate handles, binding highlights, snap lines, remote cursors, scrollbars. Owns all pointer events.
- Why: expensive rough.js element painting isolated from cheap, frequently-changing UI. Moving a handle or remote cursor repaints only interactive; scene mutation doesn't recompute overlays unless selection changed. (`NewElementCanvas.tsx` = scratch layer for element being drawn.)

**Render entry points**
- `renderer/staticScene.ts` → `renderStaticScene` → `renderStaticSceneThrottled` (`throttleRAF`) → `_renderStaticScene`: scales to zoom, `strokeGrid`, loops `visibleElements` calling `renderElement` per element + bound text + link icons.
- `renderer/interactiveScene.ts` → `renderInteractiveScene` → `_renderInteractiveScene`: overlays only; RAF-driven via `AnimationController` for animated highlights.

**Renderer + memoization** (`scene/Renderer.ts`)
- `getRenderableElements()` computes `canvasNonce` from `scene.getSceneNonce()` + viewport, delegates to `_getRenderableElements = memoize(...)` keyed on nonce + zoom/scroll/size + editing/new element. Unchanged nonce+viewport → returns **same object refs** for `elementsMap`/`visibleElements` — this drives the canvas memo compares.
- `getVisibleCanvasElements` → viewport culling (`isElementInViewport`).

**Per-element drawing + two-level cache** (`packages/element/src/`)
- `renderElement.ts` → `renderElement`: `generateElementWithCanvas` → `drawElementFromCanvas` (blit).
- Cache 1 — rough shape: `shape.ts` `ShapeCache` = `WeakMap<element, {shape,theme}>`, keyed by element identity, per-theme. Holds the rough.js `Drawable`.
- Cache 2 — offscreen bitmap: `renderElement.ts` `elementWithCanvasCache = WeakMap<element, canvas>`. Regenerated only on: zoom change, theme change, boundText version, image crop, frame opacity, arrow angle. Else the cached bitmap is blitted — rough.js never re-runs.

**Mutation → re-render chain** (end to end)
```
Scene.mutateElement(el, updates)          // element/src/Scene.ts
 → mutateElement()                        // element/src/mutateElement.ts
     • bump el.version, el.versionNonce, el.updated
     • if width/height/fileId/points changed → ShapeCache.delete(el)
         (evicts BOTH shape WeakMap + elementWithCanvasCache)
 → prevVersion !== nextVersion → Scene.triggerUpdate()
     • sceneNonce = randomInteger(); fire onUpdate callbacks
 → App.triggerRender → setState({})       // App.tsx:4655, onUpdate wired :3147
 → App.render() → renderer.getRenderableElements()   // :2091
     • new canvasNonce (sceneNonce changed) → memoize miss
     • fresh elementsMap / visibleElements refs
 → StaticCanvas.areEqual / InteractiveCanvas.areEqual fail
     (compare canvasNonce, refs, scale; interactive also selectionNonce)
 → renderStaticScene / renderInteractiveScene  (RAF-throttled)
 → renderElement → generateElementWithCanvas (cache miss → rough.js regen) → blit
```

Key subtlety: purely visual mutations (color/opacity — not width/height/fileId/points) bump version but **don't** `ShapeCache.delete`; regeneration then hinges only on `generateElementWithCanvas`'s zoom/theme/boundText/crop/frame/angle checks — otherwise cached bitmap reused. Geometry changes are the only ones that force full shape regeneration.

Files: `components/canvases/{Static,Interactive,NewElement}Canvas.tsx`, `renderer/{staticScene,interactiveScene}.ts`, `scene/Renderer.ts`, `packages/element/src/{renderElement,shape,mutateElement,Scene}.ts`, `components/App.tsx`.

## Rendering model

Excalidraw uses **two stacked `<canvas>` layers**, each a `React.memo` component that redraws only when its inputs change:

- **`StaticCanvas`** (`components/canvases/StaticCanvas.tsx`) — the elements themselves. Calls `renderStaticScene`.
- **`InteractiveCanvas`** — selection boxes, handles, remote cursors, snap lines. Calls `renderInteractiveScene`.

Splitting them means moving the selection outline never forces every shape to repaint.

## Update pipeline (element → pixels)

**1. Scene → renderable set** (`scene/Renderer.ts`)
`Renderer.getRenderableElements` pulls non-deleted elements from `Scene`, builds a `RenderableElementsMap` (drops the currently-edited text and the new-element-in-frame), then filters to **viewport-visible** elements via `isElementInViewport`. This whole step is `memoize`d on a `canvasNonce` = `scene.getSceneNonce()` (+ new-element versionNonce). Scene bumps `sceneNonce = randomInteger()` on any mutation (`Scene.ts:304`) → nonce change is the top-level "something changed, recompute" signal.

**2. React memo gate** (`StaticCanvas.areEqual`)
Re-renders only if `canvasNonce`, `scale`, the `elementsMap`/`visibleElements` refs, relevant `appState` props (`isShallowEqual` over an explicit whitelist), or `renderConfig` changed. Otherwise skips entirely.

**3. Scene draw** (`renderer/staticScene.ts` → `_renderStaticScene`)
Clears canvas, applies zoom/scroll transform, then `visibleElements.forEach(renderElement)`. Wrapped in `renderStaticSceneThrottled = throttleRAF(...)` — when throttling is on, paints at most once per animation frame (coalesces bursts).

**4. Per-element draw + caches** (`element/renderElement.ts`)
Each element is rendered from an **offscreen per-element canvas**, not redrawn from scratch:
- `elementWithCanvasCache` — `WeakMap<element, canvas>`. `generateElementWithCanvas` regenerates the element's bitmap only when zoom, theme, bound-text version, image crop, containing-frame opacity, or (arrow-label) angle changed; else returns the cached canvas. `drawElementFromCanvas` then just blits + rotates it.
- `ShapeCache` (`shape.ts`) — `WeakMap<element, roughjs drawable>`. Caches the rough.js shape generation one layer below.

So a redraw is: nonce invalidates the memoized renderable set → React memo lets the changed canvas through → RAF-throttled scene loop → each element blitted from its WeakMap-cached bitmap, regenerating only the few whose visual inputs actually changed. `freedraw`/export paths bypass the cache and draw directly.

Key files: `scene/Renderer.ts`, `scene/Scene.ts` (nonce), `renderer/staticScene.ts`, `renderer/interactiveScene.ts`, `element/renderElement.ts`, `element/shape.ts`, `components/canvases/*`.

## Data model → render

**Source of truth: `Scene`** (`packages/element/src/Scene.ts`). Holds `elements` + derived caches: `nonDeletedElements`, `nonDeletedElementsMap`, frames. Every element carries a `version` + `versionNonce`.

**Two canvases, layered** — Excalidraw splits work across separate `<canvas>`:

- **InteractiveCanvas** — selection, handles, cursors (`renderer/interactiveScene.ts`)

Separation means dragging a selection box only repaints the cheap interactive layer, not every committed element.

## Update → repaint flow

1. **Mutate.** `Scene.mutateElement(el, updates)` → `mutateElement` bumps `version`/`versionNonce` if anything actually changed. Only if version changed AND `informMutation` → `triggerUpdate()`. (`replaceAllElements` for bulk swaps rebuilds the maps, then `triggerUpdate`.)
2. **`triggerUpdate()`** — `sceneNonce = randomInteger()` + fires all `onUpdate` callbacks.
3. **React bridge.** `App` registers `this.scene.onUpdate(this.triggerRender)` (App.tsx:3147). `triggerRender()` calls `this.setState({})` — empty setState just to force a re-render (`force` variant instead calls `scene.triggerUpdate()` to also invalidate the canvas nonce).
4. **`App.render()`** → `this.renderer.getRenderableElements(...)` (`scene/Renderer.ts`). Builds the renderable `elementsMap` (drops the currently-edited text + framed new-element), computes viewport-visible elements via `isElementInViewport`. **Memoized** (`memoize` on a `canvasNonce = sceneNonce[:newElement.versionNonce]`) — same nonce + same viewport params → returns cached list, no recompute.
5. **StaticCanvas** (`React.memo`, custom `areEqual` shallow-comparing `elementsMap`/`renderConfig`) receives `elementsMap`+`visibleElements` as props; a `useEffect` calls `renderStaticScene(...)`.
6. **`renderStaticSceneThrottled = throttleRAF(...)`** — coalesces all paints to one `requestAnimationFrame`; rapid mutations in a frame collapse to a single draw (keeps only `lastArgs`).
7. **Draw.** `renderStaticScene` iterates `visibleElements` → `renderElement(...)` per element.

## Per-element caching

`renderElement` rasterizes each element to an offscreen canvas cached in a `WeakMap` keyed by the element object (`elementWithCanvasCache`, renderElement.ts:603). Reused unless invalidated by: zoom change, theme, bound-text version, image crop, containing-frame opacity, or arrow-angle-vs-label. Otherwise the cached bitmap is just blitted (`drawElementFromCanvas`). WeakMap keying means a new element version (mutation produces logically-new object) misses cache → regenerates; unchanged elements blit for free.

## Net effect
Nonce-gated memoization (skip recompute) → RAF throttle (coalesce paints) → per-element WeakMap bitmap cache (skip re-rasterize) → static/interactive layer split (repaint only what moved). A mutation touches one element's version; everything downstream reuses caches for the rest.

Django ORM query lifecycle — lazy build, single compile+execute at iteration.

## 1. QuerySet: lazy accumulator
`QuerySet` holds a `Query` object (`django.db.models.sql.query.Query`), not results. Each `.filter()`/`.exclude()/.annotate()/.order_by()` **clones** the QuerySet (and its `Query`) and mutates the clone — chaining never hits the DB. Immutability by clone is why chains are side-effect-free.

## 2. Query: the SQL-agnostic AST
`Query` accumulates state:
- `where` — a tree of nodes (`WhereNode`, `Lookup` objects). `.filter(price__gte=5)` resolves `price__gte` → field `price` + lookup `gte`, builds a `GreaterThanOrEqual` lookup node, ANDs it into `where`.
- `alias_map` / `join` — table aliases + join graph. Spanning relations (`author__name`) calls `setup_joins()` → adds `Join` entries, deduped/promoted (INNER vs LOUTER).
- `annotations`, `group_by`, `order_by`, `select`, `low_mark`/`high_mark` (slicing → LIMIT/OFFSET).

Lookups resolve against the field's `get_lookup()` / `get_transform()`, so `__gte`, `__icontains`, `__date` map to registered lookup classes that know their SQL operator.

## 3. Compilation — trigger, then `SQLCompiler`
Evaluation triggers on: iteration, `len()`, `list()`, `bool()`, slicing without step, `repr()`. Then:

`QuerySet._fetch_all()` → `Query.get_compiler(using=db)` → `SQLCompiler.execute_sql()`.

`SQLCompiler.as_sql()` assembles the string in order:
1. `pre_sql_setup()` — resolves `SELECT` columns (`get_select()`), `group_by`, `order_by`.
2. `get_from_clause()` — walks `alias_map` → `FROM main JOIN ...`.
3. `where.as_sql(compiler, connection)` — each node recurses, emitting `%s` placeholders + collecting params **separately** (never string-interpolated → parametrized, SQL-injection-safe).
4. Concatenate SELECT/FROM/WHERE/GROUP BY/HAVING/ORDER BY/LIMIT.

Backend (`connection.ops`, the vendor compiler) supplies dialect specifics — quoting, LIMIT vs `ROWNUM`, etc. Returns `(sql, params)`.

## 4. Execution + hydration
`execute_sql()` gets a cursor (`connection.cursor()`), `cursor.execute(sql, params)`. Rows come back as tuples. The compiler builds **converters** and an iterator (`results_iter` → `apply_converters`); for a model query the row → model instances via `Model.from_db()`, mapping columns to fields, wiring up `select_related` joined objects. `select_related` = extra JOINs hydrated inline; `prefetch_related` = separate queries joined in Python.

Results cache in `QuerySet._result_cache` — re-iteration is free; a re-`filter()` returns a fresh QuerySet with an empty cache.

## One-line model
`filter/annotate` → clone Query (AST) → lazy. Iterate → `SQLCompiler` walks alias_map + where tree → parametrized `(sql, params)` → `cursor.execute` → rows hydrated to objects → cached.

Key files: `db/models/query.py` (QuerySet), `db/models/sql/query.py` (Query), `db/models/sql/compiler.py` (SQLCompiler), `db/models/lookups.py` (lookups), `db/models/sql/where.py` (WhereNode).

Django ORM query lifecycle — build lazily, compile on demand, execute once, cache:

**Build (no DB hit).** A `QuerySet` wraps a `Query` object (`django.db.models.sql.query.Query`). Each `filter`/`exclude`/`annotate`/`order_by` **clones** the QuerySet and mutates the clone's `Query`:
- `add_q(Q(...))` → resolves field names against the model, adds table aliases/JOINs (`Query.table_alias`, `join()`), builds the WHERE tree as a `WhereNode` of `Lookup` nodes (e.g. `Exact`, `In`).
- Field refs become `Col` expressions bound to an alias; `annotate` adds to `.annotations`, `values()` sets the select mask.
- All lazy — clone-and-accumulate, still zero queries.

**Trigger.** DB hit only on evaluation: iteration, `list()`, `len()`, `bool()`, slicing-with-step, `repr()`, pickling. These call `QuerySet._fetch_all()`.

**Compile.** `_fetch_all` runs `self._iterable_class` (`ModelIterable` / `ValuesIterable` / `FlatValuesListIterable` …), which calls `query.get_compiler(using=db)` → `SQLCompiler` (`django.db.models.sql.compiler`). `compiler.as_sql()`:
- `pre_sql_setup` → `get_select` (SELECT cols + annotations), `get_from_clause` (FROM + JOINs from the alias map), compiles the `WhereNode` to SQL, then GROUP BY / ORDER BY / LIMIT.
- Returns `(sql_string, params)` — a **parameterized** statement; values stay separate (SQL-injection safe). Dialect specifics (quoting, LIMIT syntax, casts) come from `connection.ops` per backend.

**Execute.** `compiler.execute_sql()` gets a cursor from the connection, `cursor.execute(sql, params)`, fetches rows.

**Map + cache.** The iterable class turns each row into a model instance (or tuple/dict). Results land in `QuerySet._result_cache` — re-iteration serves the cache, no re-query.

Key files: `db/models/query.py` (QuerySet), `db/models/sql/query.py` (Query), `db/models/sql/compiler.py` (SQLCompiler), `db/models/sql/where.py` (WhereNode), `db/backends/*/operations.py` (dialect).

## Layers

`QuerySet` (`django/db/models/query.py`) is a thin, lazy wrapper. The actual query state lives in a `Query` object (`django/db/models/sql/query.py`). SQL string generation lives in a `SQLCompiler` (`django/db/models/sql/compiler.py`). Execution goes through the DB backend's cursor.

```
QuerySet  →  Query (sql.Query)  →  SQLCompiler  →  cursor.execute()  →  rows  →  model instances
   (API)      (query state tree)     (state→SQL)      (backend)                    (iterable)
```

## Build phase (lazy — no DB hit)

Each `.filter()`, `.exclude()`, `.annotate()`, `.order_by()` **clones** the QuerySet (`self._clone()`) and mutates its `Query`. Nothing touches the DB.

Inside `Query`, a filter call (`add_q` → `build_filter`) resolves each `field__lookup=value`:
- Splits on `__`, walks relations via `setup_joins` — each relation span becomes a `Join`/`BaseTable` in `Query.alias_map`, deduped and aliased (`T1`, `T2`…).
- Terminal lookup (`exact`, `gt`, `in`, `icontains`…) resolves to a `Lookup` class on the field's `output_field`.
- Builds a `WhereNode` tree of these lookups (AND/OR/NOT from `Q` objects).

So `Query` holds structured state: `alias_map` (tables/joins), `where` (WhereNode tree), `select`, `annotations`, `order_by`, `group_by`, `low_mark`/`high_mark` (slicing → LIMIT/OFFSET). No SQL yet.

## Compile phase

Triggered on **evaluation** — iteration, `len()`, `list()`, `bool()`, slicing with a step, `repr()`. `QuerySet._fetch_all()` calls the iterable class, which calls `query.get_compiler(using=db)` then `compiler.execute_sql()`.

`SQLCompiler.as_sql()` turns `Query` state into a parameterized SQL string:
- `get_from_clause()` walks `alias_map` → `FROM … JOIN …`.
- `WhereNode.as_sql()` recurses → `WHERE` with `%s` placeholders.
- `get_order_by()`, `get_group_by()`, `get_select()` build the rest.
- Returns `(sql, params)` — **values are never interpolated into the string**; they travel as a separate params tuple → the DB driver binds them (SQL-injection safe).

Each backend (`django/db/backends/*`) supplies quoting, LIMIT syntax, `DatabaseOperations` differences.

## Execute phase

`compiler.execute_sql()`:
- `cursor.execute(sql, params)` through the connection wrapper (fires `pre_`/`post` signals, connection pooling/tx state).
- Fetches rows (`cursor.fetchmany`, chunked).
- A **row-to-object** conversion — `compiler.results_iter()` + the iterable class (`ModelIterable`) maps each column tuple to a model instance via `Model.from_db`, wiring `select_related` joins and deferred fields.

Result cached on `queryset._result_cache` — re-iteration hits the cache, not the DB.

## Key consequences

- **Laziness**: chaining is free; the DB fires once, at first evaluation.
- **`.values()`/`.values_list()`** swap the iterable class (`ValuesIterable`) → dicts/tuples, skip model instantiation.
- **`.count()`/`.exists()`/`.aggregate()`** compile a different SQL shape (`SELECT COUNT(*)`, `SELECT 1 … LIMIT 1`) — don't pull rows.
- Slicing `qs[10:20]` sets `low_mark`/`high_mark` → `LIMIT/OFFSET`, still lazy; `qs[5]` with an int evaluates immediately.

Inspect any of it: `print(qs.query)` renders the compiled SQL; `str(qs.query)` calls the compiler directly.

Django ORM query lifecycle: **QuerySet → Query → SQLCompiler → cursor → model instances**. Lazy — nothing hits the DB until the QuerySet is evaluated.

**1. QuerySet — the lazy API layer** (`django/db/models/query.py`)
`Model.objects.filter(...)` returns a `QuerySet`. Each chained call (`filter`, `exclude`, `order_by`, `annotate`, `values`) returns a *new* QuerySet (cloned via `_chain()`/`_clone()`), never mutating in place, never querying. It just accumulates state on an underlying `Query` object (`qs.query`).

**2. Query — the abstract SQL representation** (`django/db/models/sql/query.py`)
Backend-agnostic tree, not SQL text. Holds:
- `where` — a `WhereNode` tree of lookups. Each `filter()` call runs `add_q()` → `build_filter()`, which splits `field__related__lookup` into joins + a lookup (`Exact`, `In`, `Gt`, `Contains`…).
- `alias_map` — table aliases + `Join` objects; relation spans auto-generate joins here.
- `select`, `annotations`, `group_by`, `order_by`, `low_mark`/`high_mark` (slicing → LIMIT/OFFSET).
Lookups resolve against the model's `Meta`/field descriptors → concrete columns.

**3. Evaluation trigger — lazy boundary**
Query runs only on: iteration (`__iter__`), `len()`, `list()`, `bool()`, slicing with a step, `repr()`, pickling. First evaluation fills `qs._result_cache`; re-iteration reuses it. `.iterator()` bypasses the cache and streams via server-side cursor (chunked).

**4. Compilation — Query → SQL** (`django/db/models/sql/compiler.py`)
Evaluation calls `query.get_compiler(using=db)` → `SQLCompiler.as_sql()`:
- `pre_sql_setup()` → `get_select()` (columns), `get_from_clause()` (tables/joins from `alias_map`), plus GROUP BY / ORDER BY.
- `where.as_sql(compiler, connection)` recurses the node tree; each lookup emits a fragment + params (`"age" > %s`, `[18]`).
- Assembles `SELECT … FROM … WHERE … ORDER BY … LIMIT …` as a string with a **separate params list** — values never interpolated into text; the driver binds them → SQL-injection safe.
Dialect differences come from `connection.ops` + `connection.features` (LIMIT syntax, quoting, etc.), which is why the same Query compiles differently per backend.

**5. Execution** (`SQLCompiler.execute_sql()`)
- `connection.cursor()` → `cursor.execute(sql, params)`. DB API driver (psycopg, mysqlclient, sqlite3) does parameter binding.
- Rows fetched (`fetchmany`/`fetchall` depending on cache vs `.iterator()`).

**6. Row → model instances**
Compiler builds `results_iter()`; each row runs through `compiler.get_converters()` (DB → Python type coercion) then a model iterable class (`ModelIterable`, or `ValuesIterable`/`FlatValuesListIterable` for `.values()`/`.values_list()`). `ModelIterable` calls `Model.from_db()` to instantiate, populating `select_related` joins inline and stashing prefetches.

**Key consequences:**
- Chaining is free; only terminal ops cost a query.
- `select_related` → JOIN (one query); `prefetch_related` → separate query + Python-side join.
- `.query` attribute or `str(qs.query)` shows compiled SQL for debugging; `connection.queries` (DEBUG=True) logs executed SQL+params.

Django ORM query lifecycle — build phase (lazy) then execute phase (on evaluation):

**1. QuerySet — lazy builder.** `Model.objects.filter(...)` returns a `QuerySet` wrapping a `Query` object (`django.db.models.sql.query.Query`). Each `filter`/`exclude`/`annotate`/`order_by` **clones** the QuerySet + mutates the clone's `Query` — no DB hit. Chaining = accumulating state on `Query`:
- `filter()` → `Query.add_q(Q(...))` → builds `WhereNode` tree of lookups (`WhereNode`/`Lookup` objects), resolving field names → `Col` expressions, auto-adding JOINs via `Query.setup_joins` (alias map in `Query.alias_map`).
- `annotate` → `Query.annotations`; `order_by` → `Query.order_by`; `values` → sets `Query.values_select`.

**2. Evaluation trigger.** QuerySet is lazy until something forces rows: iteration (`__iter__`), `len()`, `list()`, `bool()`, slicing with step, `repr()`. Triggers `QuerySet._fetch_all()` → runs the compiler, caches results in `self._result_cache`. (Slicing without step just sets `Query.low_mark`/`high_mark` — still lazy.)

**3. Compile — Query → SQL.** `Query.get_compiler(using=db)` → `SQLCompiler` (`django.db.models.sql.compiler`). `compiler.as_sql()`:
- `get_from_clause()` — JOINs from `alias_map`.
- `WhereNode.as_sql()` — walks the tree, each `Lookup.as_sql()` emits SQL fragment + params. **Params kept separate** → passed to DB driver for binding (SQL injection safe; values never string-interpolated).
- `select`, `GROUP BY`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT/OFFSET` assembled. Backend-specific SQL via `connection.ops` (each DB vendor has its own operations/compiler quirks).
- Returns `(sql_string, params_tuple)`.

**4. Execute.** `compiler.execute_sql()` → gets cursor from `connection.cursor()` → `cursor.execute(sql, params)`. Driver does parameter binding server-side.

**5. Rows → objects.** `execute_sql` fetches rows; `compiler.results_iter()` yields tuples. For model instances, `ModelIterable` maps each row → model instance via `Model.from_db`, populating fields by column position + setting up `select_related` joins inline. Deferred/`only`/`values` change which iterable + how rows map.

**Key consequences:**
- N+1: each related access on a lazy relation = new query unless `select_related` (JOIN) / `prefetch_related` (separate query + Python join).
- Re-iterating a QuerySet hits `_result_cache` — no re-query. `.iterator()` bypasses the cache (server-side cursor, streams).
- Inspect generated SQL: `str(qs.query)` (approx, not param-bound) or `qs.explain()` / `connection.queries`.

## Tokio scheduling model

**Task = spawned future.** `tokio::spawn(fut)` boxes the future into a `Task`, hands it to the runtime. Task is a state machine polled to completion. Tokio owns it via an `Arc`-like intrusive refcount (the `Harness`).

**Multi-thread runtime (default):** N worker threads, each with its own **local run queue** (fixed-size ring, 256 slots) + one shared **global (injection) queue**.

### Work-stealing scheduler
- Each worker loops: pop task from local queue → `poll()` it → repeat.
- Local queue empty → check global queue → else **steal** half the tasks from a random sibling worker's local queue.
- New `spawn` from inside a worker → pushes to that worker's local queue (hot, cache-friendly). Local queue full → overflow spills to global. `spawn` from outside the runtime → global queue.
- **LIFO slot:** each worker has a single-slot fast path for the most-recently-woken task (optimizes message-passing ping-pong: yield → immediately re-poll the woken partner). Not stealable for a short window; guarded against starvation.

### How a task gets re-polled (the wake path)
Poll returns one of:
- `Poll::Ready` → task done, dropped.
- `Poll::Pending` → task parked. Before returning Pending, the leaf future (I/O, timer, channel) stored the task's **`Waker`** in some reactor/other-task.

Task is **not** re-polled by spinning. It's inert until something calls `waker.wake()`:
- **I/O readiness:** the **reactor** (one per runtime, epoll/kqueue/IOCP via `mio`) tracks fd interest. A worker with no work calls `park()` → blocks in the epoll syscall. Readiness event → reactor looks up the `Waker` → `wake()` reschedules the task onto a run queue.
- **Timers:** hierarchical timing wheel; expiry wakes the task.
- **Channels/notify:** the sending side calls `wake()`.

`wake()` = push task back onto a run queue + unpark a sleeping worker if any.

### Fairness / preemption
- Cooperative — a future that never yields (`Poll::Pending`) blocks its worker. `.await` points are the only yield opportunities.
- **Budget:** each task gets a poll budget (~128 ops). Tokio resources (I/O, channels) decrement it; budget exhausted → the resource returns `Pending` even if ready, forcing the task to yield → prevents one busy task starving others.
- Every ~61 local-queue polls, worker checks the **global queue** first → prevents global-queue starvation.
- Blocking CPU/sync work → `spawn_blocking` (separate, unbounded-ish thread pool) so it doesn't stall a worker.

### current-thread runtime
Single thread, single queue, no stealing, no LIFO-steal concerns. `Runtime::block_on` drives the loop; parks on the reactor when idle. Same waker/budget mechanics, no cross-thread sync.

**One-line model:** tasks live on per-worker queues, workers poll them, a `Poll::Pending` parks a task until its `Waker` fires from the reactor/timer/channel, idle workers steal or park on epoll, and a per-task budget forces yielding for fairness.

## Core model
Tokio task = a `Future` polled to completion. Runtime never "runs" a task continuously — it **polls**: calls `Future::poll`, which either finishes (`Ready`) or yields (`Pending`). A task is only polled when there's reason to believe it can make progress. Tasks are cooperatively scheduled — they run until they hit an `.await` that returns `Pending`, then hand control back.

## Multi-threaded scheduler (default)
Work-stealing, one worker thread per core by default.

- **Local queues** — each worker has a fixed-size (256) LIFO local run queue. Newly woken/spawned tasks go here.
- **Global (injection) queue** — overflow from full local queues, and tasks spawned from outside a worker (e.g. from `main` or a non-runtime thread).
- **Work stealing** — a worker with an empty local queue steals ~half the tasks from another worker's queue. Keeps cores busy, balances load.
- **LIFO slot** — each worker has a single-slot "next task" optimization. When task A wakes task B (message-passing ping-pong), B goes in the LIFO slot and runs immediately next, exploiting cache locality. Guarded by a budget so it can't starve the queue.

## The poll loop (one worker)
1. Pop a task (LIFO slot → local queue → occasionally global queue → steal).
2. `poll()` it. Runs synchronously until `Pending` or `Ready`.
3. `Ready` → task dropped, output delivered to `JoinHandle`.
4. `Pending` → task parked. It is NOT requeued. It only re-enters a queue when its `Waker` is called.

## Wakers — how a Pending task comes back
The contract: when a future returns `Pending`, it must have arranged for its `Waker` to be called once progress is possible. `poll` receives a `Context` carrying the `Waker`. Leaf futures (I/O, timers, channels) stash the waker.

- **I/O**: the reactor (mio + epoll/kqueue/IOCP) registers the fd. On readiness, the driver calls the stored waker → task rescheduled onto a queue.
- **Timers**: timer wheel driver fires the waker at expiry.
- **Channels/notify**: sender calls the receiver's waker.

`wake()` = "push this task back onto a run queue." That's the whole rescheduling mechanism.

## Fairness / preventing starvation
- **Task budget** — each task gets a per-poll budget (~128) of operations. Tokio resources (I/O, channels) decrement it; when exhausted they return `Pending` (with the waker pre-armed) even if data is ready, forcing a yield. Stops a hot loop from monopolizing a worker.
- **Global queue interval** — workers periodically check the global queue instead of draining local-only, so injected tasks aren't starved.

## Blocking
Poll must never block — a blocking syscall stalls the whole worker (and its queue). Use `spawn_blocking` → runs on a separate, dynamically-sized blocking thread pool; `block_in_place` → converts a worker to a blocking thread and hands its tasks off.

## current-thread scheduler
`new_current_thread()` — single thread, single queue, no stealing, no LIFO. Same poll/waker model. Used for lightweight or `!Send` workloads.

## Mental model
Runtime = queues + worker threads. Worker = poll loop pulling ready tasks. A task is "ready" only when its waker fired. `.await` on `Pending` = park + stash waker; wake = requeue. Everything else (stealing, LIFO, budget) is scheduling policy on top of that primitive.

Tokio = multi-threaded work-stealing scheduler over a fixed thread pool (default: one worker per CPU core). Core mechanism:

**Task = spawned future.** `tokio::spawn(fut)` boxes the future into a `Task`, allocates a reference-counted heap cell (the "harness") holding the future + state, and pushes it to a run queue. A task is just a state machine polled to completion.

**Per-worker queues + global queue.** Each worker thread owns a fixed-size (256) LIFO local queue plus a single LIFO "slot". The runtime has one shared global (injection) queue. `spawn` from inside a worker → local queue; `spawn` from outside → global queue.

**Run loop.** Each worker repeatedly:
1. Every ~61 ticks (`global_queue_interval`), check global queue first — starvation guard so injected/remote tasks aren't starved by a hot local queue.
2. Pop from the LIFO slot, then local queue.
3. Local empty → **steal** half of another random worker's queue.
4. Still empty → pull a batch from global queue.
5. All empty → park the thread (epoll/kqueue via mio wakes it).

**Poll.** Running a task = calling `future.poll(cx)` with a `Waker` tied to that task's harness.
- `Poll::Ready` → task done, harness freed.
- `Poll::Pending` → future registered its `Waker` with some resource (I/O readiness in the reactor, a timer wheel, a channel). Worker drops the task and moves on — **not** busy-waited.

**Wake.** When the resource is ready, the reactor (mio-driven I/O driver / timer) calls `waker.wake()`, which re-pushes the task onto a run queue. Back to the run loop.

**LIFO slot detail.** A just-woken task goes to the slot, not the queue tail — optimizes message-passing latency (poll the woken task immediately, hot cache). One slot deep; overflow spills to the local queue. Slot tasks aren't stealable for a short window to preserve locality.

**Cooperative scheduling.** Tokio budgets each task ~128 poll-through-resource operations (`coop`); exceeding it makes the resource return `Pending` even if ready, forcing a yield so one greedy task can't monopolize a worker.

**Blocking.** CPU-bound/blocking code must not run on a worker (it stalls the state machine) — use `spawn_blocking`, which dispatches to a separate, dynamically-sized blocking thread pool.

Key point: cooperative, not preemptive — Tokio can only reschedule at `.await` points where a future yields `Pending`. A `loop {}` with no await pins a worker forever.

## Tokio scheduling model

**Task = a `Future` + state.** `tokio::spawn` boxes the future into a `Task` (a heap `Harness` holding the future, a state atomic, and a join handle), hands it to the scheduler. Task is the unit; it's a state machine polled to completion.

**Multi-threaded runtime — work-stealing scheduler.**
- One **worker thread per core** (default = num cores), each with its own **local run queue** (fixed ring buffer, 256 slots) + a shared **global (injection) queue**.
- Worker loop: pop next task from local queue → `poll()` it → repeat. Empty local queue → check global queue → still empty → **steal** half the tasks from a random sibling worker's queue → still nothing → park the thread.
- **LIFO slot**: the last-woken task goes into a single-slot fast lane, polled next before the queue. Optimizes message-passing ping-pong (task A wakes B, B runs immediately, hot cache). Anti-starvation cap limits consecutive LIFO hops.

**Poll → `Poll::Pending` → `Poll::Ready`.**
- `poll()` runs the future until it hits an `.await` that isn't ready. That await returns `Pending`; the future registers its `Waker` with whatever resource it's blocked on (I/O readiness, timer, channel), and the worker **drops the task and moves on** — the thread is never blocked.
- When the resource fires, the `Waker` is invoked → task pushed back onto a run queue (the woken worker's local queue, or global) → re-polled. A future may be polled many times; each poll resumes at the suspension point (compiler-generated state machine).

**What wakes a Pending task:**
- **I/O**: the **reactor** (one per runtime, `mio` epoll/kqueue/IOCP) runs in the worker that parks. `park()` = block on `epoll_wait`; readiness events map back to the registered Wakers.
- **Timers**: hierarchical timing wheel; expiry wakes the task.
- **Channels/notify/sync primitives**: waking the stored Waker directly.

**Cooperative scheduling / budget.** Each task gets a per-poll **budget (~128 ops)**. Tokio resources decrement it; at zero they return `Pending` even if data is ready, forcing a yield. Prevents one hot future (e.g. an always-ready socket) from starving siblings on the same worker. `tokio::task::yield_now()` yields voluntarily.

**Blocking is the failure mode.** A `poll()` that blocks (sync file read, `std::thread::sleep`, CPU loop) stalls that whole worker — its queued tasks can't run until stolen. Offload to `spawn_blocking`, which runs on a **separate, unbounded blocking-thread pool**, not the core workers.

**current-thread runtime.** Single thread, single queue, no stealing, no LIFO/sync overhead — same poll/Waker mechanics, everything on one thread.

Flow in one line: `spawn` → run queue → worker `poll()` → `Pending` registers Waker & yields thread → resource fires Waker → re-queued → re-polled → `Ready` → task drops, `JoinHandle` resolves.

Tokio runs a **multi-threaded work-stealing scheduler** (the `multi_thread` runtime; there's also a single-threaded `current_thread` flavor). Core mechanics:

**Task = a spawned future.** `tokio::spawn` boxes the future into a `Task` (a heap-allocated `Harness` holding the future + state) and hands it to the scheduler. A task is just a state machine polled to completion.

**Queues (three tiers):**
- **LIFO slot** — one per worker. The task most recently made ready goes here, not the run queue. Optimizes message-passing chains (task A wakes B, B runs immediately on the same core, cache-hot). Guarded against starvation: a task can't monopolize via the slot indefinitely.
- **Local run queue** — per worker, fixed-size ring buffer (256). Cheap, mostly lock-free push/pop by the owning worker.
- **Global (injection) queue** — shared, mutex-guarded. Overflow from full local queues and tasks spawned from outside the runtime land here.

**Worker loop.** Each worker thread runs: pop next task → `poll()` it. Poll returns `Pending` (task parks, releasing the thread) or `Ready` (done). Next-task priority roughly:
1. LIFO slot,
2. local queue,
3. periodically the global queue (every ~61 ticks, to avoid starving global/IO),
4. **steal** — half the tasks from a random sibling worker's local queue when your own is empty,
5. global queue again, then park.

**Waking / readiness.** A `Pending` future registered a `Waker`. When its resource is ready — timer fires, socket readable — the source calls `waker.wake()`, which re-inserts the task into a run queue (LIFO slot of the waking worker, or global). Only *ready* tasks consume CPU; parked ones cost nothing.

**I/O and timers.** A dedicated **driver** wraps the OS reactor (epoll/kqueue/IOCP via `mio`) plus a hierarchical timer wheel. A worker that finds no work parks *on* the driver, blocking in epoll until an event arrives, then wakes the corresponding tasks. So idle threads sleep in the kernel, not spin.

**Cooperative scheduling.** Tokio is non-preemptive — a task yields only at `.await` points. To stop a hot loop of always-ready awaits from hogging a worker, Tokio gives each task a per-poll **budget** (coop): after ~128 ready operations the resource returns `Pending` to force a yield. `tokio::task::yield_now()` yields voluntarily.

**Blocking.** CPU-bound or blocking-sync code must not run on a worker (it stalls that thread's whole queue). `spawn_blocking` offloads to a separate, elastically-sized blocking thread pool; workers stay free to poll async tasks.

Key consequence: futures must be non-blocking and yield often; a single blocking call on a worker thread degrades the whole runtime.

For the authoritative deep-dive: Tokio blog "Making the Tokio scheduler 10x faster" (Carl Lerche) and the `tokio::runtime` docs.

OkHttp request flow through `RealCall.getResponseWithInterceptorChain()`:

**Chain assembly** — interceptors composed into an ordered list, then wrapped in a `RealInterceptorChain`:
1. client's application interceptors (`OkHttpClient.interceptors()`)
2. `RetryAndFollowUpInterceptor` — retries, redirects, follow-ups
3. `BridgeInterceptor` — app request → wire request: adds `Content-Length`/`Transfer-Encoding`, `Host`, `Connection: Keep-Alive`, `Accept-Encoding: gzip`, cookies; strips gzip on response
4. `CacheInterceptor` — serves/stores response cache; short-circuits on hit
5. `ConnectInterceptor` — opens connection + `ExchangeCodec` to the target
6. client's network interceptors (`OkHttpClient.networkInterceptors()`)
7. `CallServerInterceptor` — writes request, reads response off the socket (terminal)

**Execution** — recursive, one pass down then back up:
- `chain.proceed(request)` creates the *next* `RealInterceptorChain` (index+1) and invokes `interceptor.intercept(next)`.
- Each interceptor does pre-work → calls `chain.proceed()` → does post-work on the returned `Response`. Request flows down, `Response` bubbles back up in reverse order.
- `CallServerInterceptor` is terminal — never calls `proceed`, returns the actual `Response`.

**The contract each interceptor honors** — exactly one `proceed()` per request (guarded by `calls` counter), must return non-null, must close the previous response body if it consumes then replaces it.

**Application vs network interceptor** — position drives behavior:
- application (top) — called once per `call`, sees the final response even from cache, doesn't see redirects/retries, no access to the `Connection`.
- network (below `ConnectInterceptor`) — called once per network round-trip (so multiple times across redirects/retries), sees intermediate responses, has the `Connection`, sees the exact wire request (with `BridgeInterceptor`'s added headers).

So: `interceptor.intercept()` → mutate/inspect request → `chain.proceed()` recurses to the next → hits the network → response unwinds back up through each interceptor's post-`proceed` code.

OkHttp request flow = a chain of interceptors, each wrapping the next, `RealCall` → `getResponseWithInterceptorChain()`.

**Chain assembly** (order matters — first added = outermost = runs first on request, last on response):
1. client's application interceptors (yours, `addInterceptor`)
2. `RetryAndFollowUpInterceptor` — retries, redirects, auth challenges
3. `BridgeInterceptor` — app→network headers (`Content-Length`, `gzip`, cookies, `Host`)
4. `CacheInterceptor` — serves/validates/writes cache
5. `ConnectInterceptor` — opens the connection (`ExchangeFinder` → socket/pool)
6. client's network interceptors (`addNetworkInterceptor`)
7. `CallServerInterceptor` — writes request bytes, reads response bytes (terminal)

**Mechanism** — `RealInterceptorChain`:
- `chain.proceed(request)` builds the *next* chain (index+1) and calls `interceptor.intercept(next)`.
- Each interceptor: do pre-work on the request → `next.proceed(request)` → do post-work on the returned `Response` → return it.
- Recursion unwinds: request travels down list, response travels back up in reverse.
- `index` guards: `proceed` must be called exactly once per interceptor (asserts otherwise); index increments prevent re-entry.

**Consequence of ordering:**
- Application interceptor: called once per `Call`, sees the final response even across redirects/retries, doesn't see intermediate `Content-Encoding` (gzip stripped by `BridgeInterceptor` below it), may not run if cache hit short-circuits before `ConnectInterceptor`.
- Network interceptor: runs once *per* network request (multiple on redirect), sees the raw wire request/response incl. `Connection`, gzip, and the actual socket via `chain.connection()`.

Terminal `CallServerInterceptor` never calls `proceed` — it produces the `Response` that propagates back up.

OkHttp request flow = a chain of `Interceptor` objects, each wrapping the next via `Chain.proceed(request)`.

**Structure.** `RealCall.getResponseWithInterceptorChain()` builds an ordered `List<Interceptor>`, then constructs a `RealInterceptorChain` at index 0 and calls `chain.proceed(request)`.

**Order** (out toward the network):
1. Client application interceptors (`OkHttpClient.interceptors()`) — user-added, order added.
2. `RetryAndFollowUpInterceptor` — recovery, redirects, retries; runs the whole loop.
3. `BridgeInterceptor` — app request → wire request: adds `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Host`, `Connection: keep-alive`, `Accept-Encoding: gzip`, cookies; transparent gzip decompression on the way back.
4. `CacheInterceptor` — serve from cache / conditional GET / write response to cache.
5. `ConnectInterceptor` — opens a healthy connection to the target (`RealConnection`, socket/TLS).
6. Network interceptors (`OkHttpClient.networkInterceptors()`) — user-added, see the real wire traffic.
7. `CallServerInterceptor` — writes request, reads response off the socket. The terminal interceptor — does not call `proceed`.

**Mechanism — the recursion.** `RealInterceptorChain.proceed()`:
- copies itself with `index+1` (`copy(index = index + 1, ...)`),
- calls `interceptor.intercept(next)` for the current index,
- that interceptor does pre-work → calls `next.proceed(request)` → does post-work on the returned `Response`.

So each `intercept()` body splits at the `proceed` call: code above = request going down, code below = response coming back up. Request descends indices 0→N; `CallServerInterceptor` (index N) returns a `Response`; it unwinds N→0.

**Guards.** `proceed` asserts each interceptor calls it exactly once (`calls` counter) and that the request body/connection invariants hold. An interceptor may call `proceed` multiple times (e.g. retry) only when there's no active request body being consumed.

**App vs network interceptor** — the key practical distinction:
- App interceptor: called once even if the HTTP response is served from cache or a redirect happens; doesn't see intermediate responses; operates on the original request.
- Network interceptor: sees every network round-trip (each redirect/retry), sees the exact wire request/response including `Content-Encoding: gzip`, has access to the `Connection`.

Custom interceptor = implement `Interceptor`, transform request before `proceed`, transform `Response` after — add via `addInterceptor` (app) or `addNetworkInterceptor` (network).

OkHttp request flow = a chain of `Interceptor`s wrapped around one `RealCall`, executed sequentially, each one calling the next via a `Chain`.

**Mechanism**
- `RealCall.execute()`/`enqueue()` → builds a `RealInterceptorChain` and calls `chain.proceed(request)`.
- Interceptors held in an ordered `List<Interceptor>`, assembled in `getResponseWithInterceptorChain()`:
  1. client's application interceptors (`client.interceptors`)
  2. `RetryAndFollowUpInterceptor` — recovery, redirects, auth retries
  3. `BridgeInterceptor` — app request → real HTTP request (adds `Content-Length`, `Content-Type`, `Host`, `Connection: Keep-Alive`, gzip, cookies)
  4. `CacheInterceptor` — serves/stores from cache
  5. `ConnectInterceptor` — opens connection + `ExchangeCodec` to target
  6. client's network interceptors (`client.networkInterceptors`)
  7. `CallServerInterceptor` — writes request, reads response off the wire (terminal, does not call `proceed`)

**Chain recursion** — each `RealInterceptorChain` holds `index`. `proceed(request)`:
- constructs `next = copy(index+1)`
- fetches `interceptor = interceptors[index]`
- calls `interceptor.intercept(next)`
- that interceptor does pre-work → calls `next.proceed(request)` → does post-work on the returned `Response` → returns it.

So control descends the list on the way to the network, unwinds back up on the way out — code *before* `chain.proceed()` sees the outbound request, code *after* sees the inbound response. Terminal `CallServerInterceptor` never calls `proceed` → stops the recursion.

**Guards** (`RealInterceptorChain.proceed`): each interceptor must call `proceed` exactly once (except terminal) and exactly one response body must be produced — violations throw.

**application vs network interceptor** — the practical distinction:
- application: always invoked once per call, even on cache hit or redirect; don't see intermediate redirect responses; operate on the logical request.
- network: invoked per actual network round-trip (once per redirect/retry); see `Connection`, compressed bodies, redirect 3xx responses; skipped entirely on full cache hit.

Conceptual answer (OkHttp library internals, no repo in play):

**Entry.** `call.execute()`/`enqueue()` → `RealCall.getResponseWithInterceptorChain()`. Builds an ordered `List<Interceptor>`, then a `RealInterceptorChain` positioned at index 0, calls `chain.proceed(request)`.

**Chain order** (request flows top→down, response bubbles bottom→up):
1. client's application interceptors (`addInterceptor`) — in registration order
2. `RetryAndFollowUpInterceptor` — retries, redirects, auth challenges
3. `BridgeInterceptor` — app↔network headers (gzip, cookies, `Content-Length`, `Host`, `User-Agent`)
4. `CacheInterceptor` — serves/stores per HTTP cache
5. `ConnectInterceptor` — opens/reuses a connection to the origin
6. client's network interceptors (`addNetworkInterceptor`)
7. `CallServerInterceptor` — writes request bytes, reads response (terminal — no `proceed`)

**Mechanism.** Each interceptor:
```
Response intercept(Chain chain) {
  Request request = chain.request();   // pre-processing (down the stack)
  Response response = chain.proceed(request);  // hand to next interceptor
  return response;                      // post-processing (up the stack)
}
```
`proceed` constructs a *new* `RealInterceptorChain` with `index+1` and invokes `interceptors[index].intercept(next)`. Recursive descent: each `proceed` call reaches one deeper. `CallServerInterceptor` is the base case — produces the `Response` that unwinds back up.

**Guards.** `RealInterceptorChain.proceed` enforces: each interceptor calls `proceed` exactly once (except the terminal one), and once a connection exists later interceptors don't call it more than once — throws `IllegalStateException` otherwise.

**App vs network interceptor** — the key practical distinction:
- application: called once even across redirects/retries; sees the final logical request/response; doesn't see intermediate ones; no access to `Connection`.
- network: called once per actual network round-trip (once per redirect/retry); sees `Content-Encoding: gzip` and other on-the-wire headers; has `chain.connection()`.

Gin — middleware = ordered slice + index cursor advanced by `c.Next()`.

**Storage.** Each route's handlers live in one flat `HandlersChain` (`[]HandlerFunc`). At registration, `engine.Use(mw...)` appends to the group's chain; `GET(path, h)` does `combineHandlers` = group middleware ++ route handler → one slice. So middleware and the final handler are indistinguishable — all `HandlerFunc`, all in the same array.

**Dispatch.** On a request, `Engine.ServeHTTP` grabs a pooled `Context`, radix-tree-matches method+path → sets `c.handlers = matchedChain`, `c.index = -1`, then calls `c.Next()`.

**`c.Next()` — the whole mechanism:**
```go
func (c *Context) Next() {
    c.index++
    for c.index < int8(len(c.handlers)) {
        c.handlers[c.index](c)
        c.index++
    }
}
```
Increment cursor, run handlers in order. Key: a middleware that itself calls `c.Next()` re-enters this loop *before* its own post-`Next()` code runs → nested execution. Code before `c.Next()` = pre-processing (downward); code after = post-processing (unwinds upward). Classic onion:
```go
func Logger() gin.HandlerFunc {
    return func(c *gin.Context) {
        t := time.Now()        // before
        c.Next()               // run rest of chain
        log(time.Since(t))     // after — runs on unwind
    }
}
```
A middleware that doesn't call `c.Next()` still works — the outer loop advances the index anyway, so the next handler runs regardless. `c.Next()` only matters when you need code *after* the downstream handlers.

**Abort.** `c.Abort()` sets `c.index = math.MaxInt8 (63)` → loop condition `index < len` fails → no further handlers. Doesn't unwind stack, just stops forward progress; already-entered middleware still finishes their post-`Next()` code. Hence the 63-handler chain limit (`abortIndex int8 = math.MaxInt8`).

**Verified:** mechanism per gin source `context.go` (`Next`/`Abort`/`index`), `gin.go` (`ServeHTTP`, `handleHTTPRequest`), `routergroup.go` (`combineHandlers`).

Gin's middleware = a slice, not a stack. Mechanism:

**Chain assembly.** Each route stores `HandlersChain` = `[]HandlerFunc` — the group's middleware + the final handler, concatenated at registration time (`group.combineHandlers`). Radix tree per HTTP method; on match, `Engine.handleHTTPRequest` sets `c.handlers = node.handlers` and `c.index = -1`, then calls `c.Next()`.

**Execution — `Context.Next()`:**
```go
func (c *Context) Next() {
    c.index++
    for c.index < int8(len(c.handlers)) {
        c.handlers[c.index](c)
        c.index++
    }
}
```
Index-driven, not recursive-by-framework. Each handler runs in order. A middleware that wants "before/after" logic calls `c.Next()` *itself* mid-body — that re-enters the loop, runs all downstream handlers to completion, then unwinds back to the code after `c.Next()`. Middleware that doesn't call `c.Next()` just falls through to the next one via the loop (still runs everything after it).

**Short-circuit — `c.Abort()`:** sets `c.index = abortIndex` (63). Loop condition fails → no further handlers run. `AbortWithStatus` etc. wrap this. Abort does *not* unwind Go's stack — code after `c.Next()` in already-entered middleware still runs.

Key consequences:
- "after" logic = put it *after* `c.Next()` in your middleware (e.g. logging latency, setting headers pre-write).
- Ordering = registration order; global `engine.Use(...)` middleware prepended to every route's chain.
- `int8` index caps a chain at 63 handlers (abortIndex sentinel).
- 404/405 route to `engine.allNoRoute`/`allNoMethod` chains — global middleware still applied.

- `c.Abort()` sets `c.index = 63` (`abortIndex`) → loop condition fails → remaining handlers skipped. Note: current handler keeps running after `Abort()`; abort only blocks *subsequent* ones, so `return` after `c.Abort()` if you want to stop immediately.

Key consequences:
- A middleware that never calls `c.Next()` still lets the chain continue — `Next()`'s outer loop advances the index regardless once the handler returns. Calling `c.Next()` explicitly only matters when you need post-hooks *after* downstream handlers.

**Chain assembly.** Each route = `HandlersChain` ([]HandlerFunc) = global middleware (from `Use`) + group middleware + the route's final handler, concatenated at registration time. Stored in the radix tree node for that method+path.

**`Context.Next()` drives the chain:**
```go
func (c *Context) Next() {
    c.index++
    for c.index < int8(len(c.handlers)) {

        c.index++
    }
}
```
Index-based iteration, not callback nesting. Each handler runs in order. A middleware that wants "before/after" logic calls `c.Next()` *itself* mid-body:
```go
func Logger(c *gin.Context) {
    t := time.Now()      // before
    c.Next()             // run rest of chain
    log(time.Since(t))   // after — unwinds back here
}
```
The inner `Next()` advances `index` through the remaining handlers; when they return, control unwinds to the code after `c.Next()`. Middleware that omits `c.Next()` still works — the outer loop advances to the next handler anyway (so `Next()` matters only when you need post-processing after downstream).

**Abort.** `c.Abort()` sets `c.index = 63` (math.MaxInt8), so the loop condition fails and no further handlers run. Already-entered middleware still finish their post-`Next()` code. `AbortWithStatus` = `Abort` + status.

Key consequences:
- Middleware ordering = registration order; `Use` before route definition.
- Single shared `Context` across the whole chain — `c.Set/Get` passes data between middleware.

Gin precomputes a **flat handler slice** per route, then walks it with an index cursor. No linked list, no per-request wrapping.

**Registration (build time)**
- `engine.Use(mw...)` → appends to `RouterGroup.Handlers`.
- Registering a route: `combineHandlers` allocates one `HandlersChain` (`[]HandlerFunc`) = group middleware **+** route handler, copied into a single contiguous slice. Chain is fixed at startup, not assembled per request.
- Stored in a per-method radix tree (httprouter-style prefix trees, one tree per HTTP verb).

**Dispatch (request time)** — `Engine.handleHTTPRequest`:
1. Pick tree by method, walk radix tree to match path, extract params.
2. Node yields the precomputed chain → `c.handlers = chain`, `c.index = -1`.
3. `c.Next()`.

**The chain walk** — `Context.Next`:
```go
func (c *Context) Next() {
    c.index++
    for c.index < int8(len(c.handlers)) {
        c.handlers[c.index](c)
        c.index++
    }
}
```
Index lives on the `Context` (an `int8`). The loop drives forward. "Around" (nesting) behavior comes from a handler calling `c.Next()` *itself*: that re-enters `Next`, advances the cursor deep into downstream handlers, they run to completion, then control unwinds back and the handler resumes its post-`Next()` code. A middleware that never calls `Next()` still doesn't stop the chain — the outer loop just advances to the next handler when it returns. Calling `Next()` only changes *ordering* (pre/post split around downstream), not whether downstream runs.

**Stopping** — `c.Abort()` sets `c.index = abortIndex` (`63`), so the `for` guard fails and no further handlers run. `int8` cursor caps a chain at 63 handlers (`abortIndex = math.MaxInt8/2`). `AbortWithStatus` etc. = abort + write status.

Net: routing = radix-tree match → one flat slice; middleware = that slice walked by an index, with `Next()` for wrap-around and `Abort()` to short-circuit.

Not in this workspace (Python repo). Answering from Alamofire's architecture (v5).

**Build → Send → Validate pipeline**

**1. Build**
- Entry: `AF.request(_:method:parameters:encoding:headers:)` on `Session` (the successor to `Manager`). Returns a `DataRequest` synchronously; no network yet.
- `Session` wraps a `URLSession` + `SessionDelegate`. It holds a `rootQueue` (serial, all internal state mutations), `requestQueue`, and `serializationQueue`.
- The call creates a `RequestConvertible`/`RequestEncodableConvertible` box holding URL, method, params, `ParameterEncoding` (or `ParameterEncoder` for `Encodable`), and headers.
- `Session.perform(_:)` dispatches async onto `rootQueue`. There it asks the convertible for a `URLRequest` via `asURLRequest()`:
  - builds base `URLRequest` (url + method + headers),
  - applies encoding — `URLEncoding` (query string or form body) or `JSONEncoding` (serializes body, sets `Content-Type`).
  - Any throw here → request finishes with `.createURLRequestFailed`.
- Then each `RequestInterceptor.adapt(_:for:completion:)` runs — mutates the `URLRequest` (auth headers, etc.) before it's sent. Result becomes the `URLRequest` used for the `URLSessionTask`.

**2. Send**
- On `rootQueue`, `Request.task(for:using:)` creates the actual `URLSessionDataTask` from the adapted `URLRequest`, and `SessionDelegate` maps the task back to the `Request` (via a `[task: Request]` table).
- `task.resume()` fires it (unless the `Request` is suspended). `URLSession` does the transport.
- `SessionDelegate` receives `URLSessionDataDelegate` callbacks — appends `Data`, tracks metrics/progress, and on `didCompleteWithError` records the terminal `URLSessionTask` result.
- `RequestInterceptor.retry(_:for:dueTo:completion:)` gets consulted on failure → `.retry`/`.retryWithDelay`/`.doNotRetry`. Retry rebuilds a fresh task through the same adapt path.

**3. Validate**
- `.validate()` on the `DataRequest` doesn't run immediately — it appends a `Validation` closure to the request's validators list.
- When the task completes, validators run on the `rootQueue` before serialization: default `validate()` checks
  - status code in `200..<300`,
  - `Content-Type` against the request's `Accept` header.
- Custom `.validate { request, response, data in ... }` returns `.success` or `.failure(Error)`.
- First failing validator sets the request's `error`; that error propagates into every response handler.

**4. Deliver**
- `.responseDecodable(of:)` / `.responseData` / `.responseString` append a `ResponseSerializer`. On completion Alamofire runs serialization on `serializationQueue`, wraps everything in `DataResponse<Success, Failure>` (request, response, data, metrics, result), then calls your completion on the queue you specify (default `.main`).

One-line mental model: `Session.request` → box → `asURLRequest()` (encode) → `adapt` → `URLSessionTask.resume` → delegate collects data → validators → serializer → `DataResponse`. Everything mutating state is funneled through the serial `rootQueue`.

## Build
- Entry: `Session.request(_:method:parameters:encoding:headers:interceptor:)` — or pass a `URLRequestConvertible` directly (e.g. a `Router` enum).
- Convenience args get wrapped in a `RequestConvertible`/`RequestEncodableConvertible` that conforms to `URLRequestConvertible`. Its `asURLRequest()`:
  1. builds a base `URLRequest` from URL + method + headers,
  2. runs a **`ParameterEncoding`** (`URLEncoding`, `JSONEncoding`) or the newer **`ParameterEncoder`** (`URLEncodedFormParameterEncoder`, `JSONParameterEncoder`) to fold parameters into the request — query string vs. httpBody depending on encoder/method.
- Result is a `DataRequest` (or `DownloadRequest`/`UploadRequest`/`DataStreamRequest`) added to the `Session`.

## Send
- All state mutation runs on the Session's serial `rootQueue` for thread safety.
- `Request` gathers its initial `URLRequest`, then the **`RequestInterceptor`** pipeline runs `adapt(_:for:completion:)` — last chance to mutate (auth headers, etc.).
- Adapted request → `URLSessionTask` created on the Session's `URLSession`. The **`SessionDelegate`** receives all `URLSession` delegate callbacks and routes each to the owning `Request` via a `requestTaskMap`.
- Task `resume()`s (unless the request was created suspended). Response body accumulates on the delegate; metrics/errors captured.
- On task completion, **response serialization** (`.response`, `.responseDecodable(of:)`, `.responseData`, …) runs on a serialization queue, producing an `AFDataResponse<T>`.
- Failures can re-enter the interceptor's `retry(_:for:dueTo:completion:)` → `.retry` / `.retryWithDelay` / `.doNotRetry`, looping back to send.

## Validate
- `.validate()` inserted in the chain before serialization appends a `Validation` closure. Default checks:
  - **status code** in `200..<300`,
  - **Content-Type** compatible with the request's `Accept` header.
- Custom: `.validate { request, response, data in ... }` returning `.success`/`.failure(error)`, or `.validate(statusCode:)` / `.validate(contentType:)`.
- A failed check yields `AFError.responseValidationFailed(reason:)`, which propagates into the serialized response's `.result` (and can trigger retry).

Chain shape: `session.request(...).validate().responseDecodable(of: T.self) { response in ... }` — validate runs on the task result, serialization only proceeds on a passing validation.

Alamofire isn't in this workspace (this is a Python repo) — answering from the library's design.

**Build**
- `Session` (default `AF`) is the entry point; wraps a `URLSession` + delegate + a serial `rootQueue`.
- `AF.request(url, method:parameters:encoding:headers:)` → creates a `RequestConvertible`/`RequestEncodableConvertible` that captures the params.
- On the `rootQueue`, `Session.perform` asks the convertible for a `URLRequest`:
  - `URLRequest` built from `URLComponents` (url, method, headers).
  - `ParameterEncoding` (`URLEncoding`/`JSONEncoding`) or `ParameterEncoder` (`URLEncodedFormParameterEncoder`/`JSONParameterEncoder`) mutates it — query string vs body, sets `Content-Type`.
  - `RequestAdapter` (via `RequestInterceptor`) gets a last async pass — inject auth headers, sign, etc.
- Result wrapped in a `DataRequest` (subclass of `Request`), state = `.initialized`.

**Send**
- `Session` calls `request.didCreateURLRequest`, then builds a `URLSessionTask` from the `URLRequest` and resumes it (respects `startRequestsImmediately`).
- The `SessionDelegate` receives `URLSession` callbacks on the delegate queue, routes them back to the `Request` on its `underlyingQueue`; bytes accumulate in the `DataRequest`'s buffer.
- `RequestInterceptor.retry` decides on failure: `.retry` / `.retryWithDelay` re-runs the whole adapt→task cycle, or `.doNotRetry`.
- Task completes → `Request` transitions to `.finished`.

**Validate**
- `.validate()` appends a closure to the request's `validators`; multiple allowed. Two built-in forms:
  - status code — default acceptable is `200..<300`; `.validate(statusCode:)` for a custom set.
  - `Content-Type` — matches response MIME against the request's `Accept` header (or an explicit list).
  - `.validate { request, response, data in ... }` for a custom `ValidationResult` (`.success`/`.failure(Error)`).
- Validators run before the response serializer. A failure sets the request's `error` to `AFError.responseValidationFailed(reason:)`.

**Serialize/deliver**
- `.responseDecodable(of:)` / `.responseData` / `.responseString` append a serializer that runs on a `serializationQueue` after validation.
- Serializer sees the validation error first (short-circuits), else decodes the buffered data.
- Final `AFDataResponse<T>` (request, response, data, metrics, `Result<T, AFError>`) dispatched to the caller's `queue` (default `.main`).

Typical chain:
```swift
AF.request("https://api.example.com/users",
           parameters: ["page": 1],
           encoding: URLEncoding.default)
  .validate(statusCode: 200..<300)
  .validate(contentType: ["application/json"])
  .responseDecodable(of: [User].self) { response in
      switch response.result {
      case .success(let users): …
      case .failure(let error): …   // AFError
      }
  }
```

Core pipeline: `convertible → URLRequest → ParameterEncoder → RequestAdapter → URLSessionTask → SessionDelegate → validators → serializer → AFDataResponse`. Interceptor sits at both adapt (outbound) and retry (failure) points; everything is marshaled through the `Session`'s serial `rootQueue` for thread-safety.

Not in workspace — answering from the library's architecture (Alamofire 5.x).

## Build

`Session` is the entry point (`AF` = `Session.default`). Call chain:

- `Session.request(_:method:parameters:encoding:headers:...)` — or the trailing-closure `RequestConvertible` form.
- Inputs collapse into a `URLRequestConvertible`. Alamofire wraps them in `RequestConvertible`/`RequestEncodableConvertible`, whose `asURLRequest()` does two things: builds a base `URLRequest` from URL+method+headers, then runs a `ParameterEncoding` (`URLEncoding`, `JSONEncoding`) or, in the `Encodable` path, a `ParameterEncoder` (`URLEncodedFormParameterEncoder`, `JSONParameterEncoder`) to inject params into query string or body.
- `Session` creates a `DataRequest` (subclass of `Request`), assigns it an `id` (UUID), and hands it to the internal queue.

## Send

- Work is serialized on `Session.rootQueue` (a serial `DispatchQueue`); user callbacks fire on a separate `underlyingQueue`/target queue. `requestQueue` and `serializationQueue` split request setup from response parsing.
- `perform(_:)` → the request's `URLRequestConvertible.asURLRequest()` runs (can throw → request finishes with `.createURLRequestFailed`).
- **RequestAdapter** (from the `RequestInterceptor`) gets a chance to mutate the `URLRequest` — attach auth headers, etc. — via `adapt(_:for:completion:)`.
- Adapted request → `URLSession` task created (`URLSessionDataTask` for `DataRequest`). Alamofire's `SessionDelegate` is the `URLSessionDelegate`; it routes delegate callbacks back to the matching `Request` by task.
- `resume()` starts the task. Response bytes accumulate; on completion `SessionDelegate` collects `data`, `URLResponse`, `error` into the `Request`.

## Validate

- `.validate()` appends a validation closure to the request's `validators` (you can also pass a custom `Validation`). Default `validate()` checks:
  - **status code** acceptable range — `200..<300`.
  - **Content-Type** — response MIME against the request's `Accept` header (when present).
- Validators run before response serialization. Failure produces `AFError.responseValidationFailed(reason:)`, which becomes the request's error and is surfaced to the response handler.

## Retry / finish

- On error (network, adapt, or validation), the interceptor's **RequestRetrier** `retry(_:for:dueTo:completion:)` decides `.retry` / `.retryWithDelay` / `.doNotRetry`. Retry re-enters `perform` from the adapt stage.
- Terminal: response serializers (`responseDecodable`, `responseData`, `responseString`) run on `serializationQueue`, wrap outcome in `AFDataResponse<T>` (a `Result`), dispatch to the caller's queue.

One request's full path: `AF.request(...).validate().responseDecodable(of: T.self) { response in ... }` → build `URLRequest` → adapt → `URLSessionDataTask` → collect → validate → serialize → callback.

Not this repo — lemoncrow is Python, no extension-host concept. Answering the general VS Code architecture question.

**Extension host ↔ main process** in VS Code:

- **Separate process.** Extension host runs in its own Node.js process (`extensionHost.ts`), spawned by the main/renderer. Isolates extension crashes/CPU from UI.
- **Transport.** A duplex message channel over the process boundary — Electron `MessagePort`/IPC (or a socket for remote/server scenarios). Bytes, not shared memory.
- **Protocol.** Custom **RPC** layer, not raw messages:
  - `RPCProtocol` (`extHost.protocol.ts`) multiplexes calls over one channel.
  - Two proxy families, mirror images: **main thread** exposes `MainThreadX` services; **ext host** exposes `ExtHostX`. Each side holds typed proxies (`getProxy(ctx.MainContext.MainThreadEditors)` etc.) generated from identifier tables.

The extension host runs in a **separate process** (or a Web Worker in the browser). It never touches the workbench UI directly — all communication happens over an **asynchronous, message-passing RPC protocol**. There are three layers:

All three converge on the tiny `IMessagePassingProtocol` interface (`src/vs/base/parts/ipc/common/ipc.ts:99`):

```ts

  send(buffer: VSBuffer): void;
  readonly onMessage: Event<VSBuffer>;
  drain?(): Promise<void>;
}
```

So everything above the transport just sends/receives opaque `VSBuffer` byte blobs — the transport details are abstracted away.

## 2. The RPC layer

Both sides construct an `RPCProtocol` (`src/vs/workbench/services/extensions/common/rpcProtocol.ts:117`) over that protocol. **Calling a remote method** — `getProxy(identifier)` returns a JS `Proxy` (`rpcProtocol.ts:251`). Any `$`-prefixed method access is trapped and turned into `_remoteCall(rpcId, method, args)`, which:
1. Serializes the arguments (`MessageIO.serializeRequest`), assigns an incrementing `req` id.
2. Stores a `LazyPromise` in `_pendingRPCReplies[req]`.
3. Sends the bytes via `this._protocol.send(msg)`.

**Receiving** — `_receiveOneMessage` (`rpcProtocol.ts:282`) reads a one-byte `MessageType` + `req` id and dispatches. For a request it calls `_invokeHandler` → `_doInvokeHandler`, which looks up the local actor in `this._locals[rpcId]` and invokes the real method, then serializes the resolved value back as a `ReplyOK*` (or `ReplyErr`) message. - **Acknowledgements + responsiveness tracking** — each request is acked; if acks don't arrive within `UNRESPONSIVE_TIME` (3s), the ext host is flagged `Unresponsive` (this is what surfaces the "extension host unresponsive" UI).

## How the extension host talks to the main process

### 1. The transport: `IMessagePassingProtocol`

export interface IMessagePassingProtocol {
    send(buffer: VSBuffer): void;
    readonly onMessage: Event<VSBuffer>;
    drain?(): Promise<void>;
}
```

It only moves raw `VSBuffer` bytes — it knows nothing about extensions or method calls. The concrete implementation depends on the host type:
- **Local desktop extension host** (`localProcessExtensionHost.ts`) — a Node child process connected via an Electron **MessagePort** (`acquirePort` / `MessagePortExtHostConnection`).
- **Web worker extension host** — a `Worker` MessageChannel.
- **Remote extension host** (`remoteExtensionHost.ts`) — a **socket** (WebSocket/TCP) to a remote server, wrapped by `PersistentProtocol` in `ipc.net.ts`.

### 2. The RPC engine: `RPCProtocol`

    return this._remoteCall(rpcId, name, myArgs);
};
```

**Incoming messages** — `_receiveOneMessage` (line 282) decodes the buffer, and for requests dispatches through `_receiveRequest → _invokeHandler`, which looks up the target actor in `_locals` and calls the real method. Replies flow back the same way (`_receiveReply` / `_receiveReplyErr`).

- **Simple/JSON args** — when all arguments are plain, everything is one `JSON.stringify`.
- **Mixed args** — used when any argument is a `VSBuffer`, `undefined`, or a `SerializableObjectWithBuffers`. Each argument is encoded individually so binary buffers ride alongside JSON without base64 overhead.

This also handles responsiveness monitoring: a request that isn't acknowledged within `UNRESPONSIVE_TIME` (3s) flips the host into an "unresponsive" state, and cancellation tokens map to `Cancel` messages.

## Overview: a two-layer canvas + memoized render pipeline

Excalidraw does **not** use React to draw shapes. Shapes are painted imperatively onto `<canvas>` elements. React only manages the DOM/UI chrome and decides *when* to hand fresh element data to the canvas painters. There are two stacked canvases:

- **Static canvas** — committed elements (`renderStaticScene`)
- **Interactive canvas** — selection boxes, handles, remote cursors (`renderInteractiveScene`)
- Plus a **NewElementCanvas** used as an optimization while you're actively drawing a shape.

## The data model: `Scene`

`Scene` (`packages/element/src/Scene.ts`) is the source of truth. It holds `elements`, an `elementsMap`, derived non-deleted collections, and a `sceneNonce`. The key mechanism is a callback/nonce pattern:

- `replaceAllElements()` rebuilds the maps and calls `triggerUpdate()` (Scene.ts:271).
- `triggerUpdate()` (Scene.ts:303) generates a fresh random `sceneNonce` and fires every registered callback. `App` registers a callback that calls `setState`, so a scene change → React re-render.

## Updating an element: `mutateElement`

There are three layers, and which one you call determines whether the UI repaints:

1. **`mutateElement(element, elementsMap, updates)`** (`mutateElement.ts:37`) — the low-level primitive. It mutates the element **in place**, diffs each key to skip no-op writes, invalidates the `ShapeCache` when geometry changes (width/height/points/fileId, line 136), and bumps `version`, `versionNonce`, and `updated`. Its docstring explicitly warns: *this won't trigger a re-render.*
2. **`Scene.mutateElement(...)`** (Scene.ts:411) — wraps the above, then, only if the element is actually in the scene, its version changed, and `informMutation` is set, calls `triggerUpdate()` to schedule a repaint.
3. **`App.mutateElement` / `ExcalidrawImperativeAPI.mutateElement`** (App.tsx:4644) — the public entry point, delegating to `scene.mutateElement`.

There's also `newElementWith` (immutable copy, used where new object identity is needed) and `bumpVersion` (version bump only).

`version`/`versionNonce` exist for collaboration conflict resolution; `sceneNonce` exists to cheaply detect "did anything change" for render memoization.

## From state change to pixels

1. A scene update fires the Scene callback → `App.setState` → React re-renders `App.render()` (App.tsx:2091).
2. `render()` calls `this.renderer.getRenderableElements({...})` (App.tsx:2104) passing zoom, scroll, viewport size, and the editing/new element.
3. **`Renderer.getRenderableElements`** (`Renderer.ts:208`) builds a `canvasNonce` from the scene nonce and calls the **memoized** `_getRenderableElements` (Renderer.ts:159). Because it's `memoize`d on `canvasNonce` + viewport params, an unrelated re-render returns the cached result and does zero recomputation. When it does run, it:
   - filters out the element being text-edited and the new-element-being-drawn (`getRenderableElementsMap`),
   - computes `visibleElements` via viewport culling — `getVisibleCanvasElements` keeps only elements passing `isElementInViewport` (Renderer.ts:47).
4. `render()` passes `elementsMap` / `visibleElements` / `canvasNonce` as **props** to the `<StaticCanvas>` and `<InteractiveCanvas>` React components.

- **`<InteractiveCanvas>`** — selection boxes, handles, remote cursors, snap lines.

- **Nonce-based memoization** at two levels (`Renderer` memoize + per-canvas nonce checks) prevents redundant work — a re-render that changed nothing paints nothing.
